Intermediate (B1) Conversation Lesson Students

B1: Shopping Mall English

Available levels
A2B1

Source topic: Shopping mall English: food court, groceries, and checkout

Quality checked lesson

A B1 practical mall English lesson that levels up the earlier A2 version with more realistic choices, polite follow-up questions, grocery availability language, and a small grammar lane for polite requests and some/any.

Transcript

Choose how to use this lesson

You can switch anytime.

Current mode

Study mode: full transcript with word help and inline checks.

Study Layout

Experience

Study Mode

Grammar

Use the same dialogue again, but focus on the grammar patterns inside it.

Grammar Focus

Choose one target, then switch highlights on to see it inside the dialogue.

Polite request phrases like I'd like, Could I, Can I, and Would you like.

Polite Requests

A2 · practical

Use polite request phrases when ordering, asking for help, or paying.

I'd like | Could I | Can I | Would you like

"Could I have orange juice with it?"

Use Could I for a polite question or request.

Jump to transcript

"Also, can I pay by card?"

Use Can I for a direct practical question in a real situation.

Jump to transcript

Choose

_____ have orange juice with it?

Show answer

Could I

Tip: Use Could I for a polite question or request.

Jump to transcript

Fill in

_____ get a smaller bag, please?

Show answer

Could I

Tip: Use Could I to complete the line.

Jump to transcript

Rebuild

can / I / pay / by / card / Also,

Show answer

Also, can I pay by card?

Also okay: Also, can I pay by card

Tip: Put the words back into a natural sentence.

Jump to transcript

Some / Any

A2 · practical

Use some and any when asking about items, quantities, or what is available.

some | any

"Hello, do you have any milk, bread, eggs, and bananas?"

Use any in many questions and negative answers about availability.

Jump to transcript

Choose

Hello, do you have _____ milk, bread, eggs, and bananas?

Show answer

any

Tip: Use any in many questions and negative answers about availability.

Jump to transcript

Say It Yourself

  • Say the same idea more politely: "Could I have orange juice with it?".
  • Make one new shopping sentence from this line: "Hello, do you have any milk, bread, eggs, and bananas?".
  • Ask one more short shopping or food question politely.
  • Say what you still need to buy, order, or check before you leave.

How to use this lesson

Tap a word for help. Click a sentence to jump the audio. Pause after the prompts and answer before you reveal.

?

Read the sentence again. How long does the light meal take to be ready?

You can start like this: It takes about...

Talk together

They ask for directions and work out where to go next in the mall.

Do you ask for help in a mall?

Start like this:Yes, I do sometimes.

Continue The Conversation

Ask for directions first

Continue as You.

Answer as yourself. Ask for the floor, add one follow-up question, and say why that helps you move faster in a big mall.

Focus: polite requests

Tip: Use one polite question and one short reason.

Quick Grammar Check

_____ have orange juice with it?

Choose the polite phrase that fits the situation.

I'd likeCould ICan IWould you like
Check answer

Could I

Tip: Use Could I for a polite question or request.

Show starter phrases
  • What floor is it on?
  • Could you tell me what floor it is on?
  • Is it near the cinema?
  • I ask for the floor first because...
Show one model reply

First, I ask what floor it is on, and then I ask if it is near the cinema because that gives me a fast mental map.

Continue The Conversation

Compare your plan

Continue as You.

Explain your mall plan in 3 short parts: where you would go first, what you would eat or buy, and why that order makes sense.

Tip: At B1, a stronger answer can compare two options and explain one clear choice.

Show starter phrases
  • Yes, I ask when...
  • I look for...
  • The directions are easier when...
  • I would like...
  • I usually order...
  • I like... because...
Show one model reply

I'd go to the food court first because I don't like shopping when I'm hungry. After lunch, I'd buy groceries and pay by card before I leave.

More acceptable replies
  • I would check the supermarket first if I only needed groceries, but I would eat first on a long shopping day.
  • I would ask for directions first, then buy lunch, because I like to know the plan before I start.

Check your understanding

?

After the first question, what follow-up question can a person ask?

You can start like this: A good follow-up question is...

Talk together

They keep the conversation moving by talking about future plans instead of stopping at the past trip.

Where do you want to go on your next vacation?

Start like this:Next time, I want to...

?
